messages.content: If you are a SCI paper author, help me revise this paragraph to make it more logical：NK cells are a unique subset of immune cells that have been shown to play an important role in the regulation of fibrotic processes. They are able to recognize and kill activated or senescent HSC through their activating receptors, such as NKG2D. In addition, NK cells produce IFN-γ and release cytotoxic particles, leading to the elimination of fibrotic cells. Therefore, therapeutic measures targeting NK cells have shown promise in reducing liver fibrosis. To explore whether the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 involves NK cell regulation, we initially examined the distribution of NK cells in the liver. NK cells were found to accumulate more in the livers of IL-10-treated fibrotic mice, predominantly in proximity to fibrous septa. Moreover, when using NKG2D, an activating receptor, as a marker for activated NK cells, immunofluorescence images showed a significant increase in the number and activity of NK cells in the livers of IL-10-intervened fibrotic mice. Furthermore, we isolated mouse liver lymphocytes and conducted a more detailed analysis of NK cells. Flow cytometry revealed that IL-10 gene intervention could enhance the frequency of NK cells in fibrotic tissues, leading to the upregulation of NKG2D expression and increased secretion of IFN- γ. Additionally, there was an elevated production of CD107a. These findings strongly suggest that NK cells, with enhanced functionality, play a crucial role in the antifibrotic process mediated by IL-10.

Revised paragraph:

NK cells are a unique subset of immune cells that have demonstrated their significance in regulating fibrotic processes. They achieve this through the recognition and elimination of activated or senescent HSC via their activating receptors, such as NKG2D. Additionally, NK cells contribute to the elimination of fibrotic cells through the production of IFN-γ and the release of cytotoxic particles. Given the promising results of therapeutic measures targeting NK cells in reducing liver fibrosis, we sought to investigate whether IL-10, known for its anti-fibrotic effect, also involves the regulation of NK cells. Initially, we examined the distribution of NK cells in the liver. Our findings revealed an accumulation of NK cells in the livers of IL-10-treated fibrotic mice, particularly in close proximity to fibrous septa. Furthermore, immunofluorescence images using NKG2D as a marker for activated NK cells showed a significant increase in both the number and activity of NK cells in the livers of IL-10-intervened fibrotic mice. To delve deeper, we isolated mouse liver lymphocytes for a more detailed analysis of NK cell functionality. Flow cytometry analysis demonstrated that IL-10 gene intervention enhanced the frequency of NK cells in fibrotic tissues. This led to the upregulation of NKG2D expression, increased secretion of IFN-γ, and elevated production of CD107a. Taken together, these findings strongly support the vital role of NK cells, with enhanced functionality, in the IL-10-mediated antifibrotic process.

If you are a SCI paper author, help me revise this paragraph to make it more concise and logical：NK cells are a unique subset of immune cells that have been shown to play an important role in the regulation of fibrotic processes. They are able to recognize and kill activated or senescent HSC through their activating receptors, such as NKG2D. In addition, NK cells produce IFN-γ and release cytotoxic particles, leading to the elimination of fibrotic cells. Therefore, therapeutic measures targeting NK cells have shown promise in reducing liver fibrosis. To explore whether the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10 involves NK cells, we initially examined the distribution of NK cells in the liver. Activated NK cells were found to accumulate intensively in the livers of IL-10-treated fibrotic mice, predominantly in fibrous septa. To delve deeper, we isolated liver lymphocytes for a more detailed analysis of NK cell functionality.
Flow cytometry analysis demonstrated that IL-10 gene intervention led to an increase in the frequency of NK cells in fibrotic tissues, as well as an upregulation of NKG2D expression and an increase in the production of IFN-γ and CD107a. Taken together, these findings strongly support NK cells, with enhanced functionality, play a crucial role in the antifibrotic process mediated by IL-10.

Revised paragraph:

NK cells are a unique subset of immune cells with a crucial role in regulating fibrotic processes. They can recognize and eliminate activated or senescent HSC through activating receptors like NKG2D, while also releasing IFN-γ and cytotoxic particles to eliminate fibrotic cells. Therapeutic approaches targeting NK cells have shown promise in reducing liver fibrosis. To investigate the involvement of NK cells in the anti-fibrotic effect of IL-10, we examined their distribution in the liver. Notably, activated NK cells accumulated significantly in the livers of IL-10-treated fibrotic mice, primarily in fibrous septa. In-depth analysis was performed by isolating and studying liver lymphocytes. Flow cytometry revealed that IL-10 gene intervention increased the frequency of NK cells in fibrotic tissues. This was accompanied by upregulated NKG2D expression, increased secretion of IFN-γ, and elevated production of CD107a. Collectively, these findings strongly support the essential role of NK cells, enhanced in functionality, in IL-10-mediated antifibrotic processes.
improve：Taken together, these findings strongly support that the enhanced functional NK cells play a crucial role in the antifibrotic process mediated by IL-10.
Enhanced functional NK cells play a critical role in the antifibrotic process mediated by IL-10, as strongly supported by these findings.

If you are a SCI paper author, help me revise this paragraph to make it more concise and logical：The immune function of liver NK cells is closely related to their development and source subpopulations. According to the level of development, they can be divided into three stages: ink (cd27+cd11b-) with low cytotoxicity and high proliferation, TNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and high proliferation, and MNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and low proliferation. Our results showed that IL-10 gene intervention increased the frequency of TNK cells, which may be related to enhancing total NK cytotoxicity. In addition, NK cells are divided into two groups according to their sources. One is liver resident NK cells, which play an important role in liver homeostasis and tissue repair in the liver. The other is peripheral circulating CNK cells, which can migrate from the blood to the liver during inflammation or infection and exert cytotoxic activity on infected or transformed cells. We observed that the frequency of lrnk cells did not differ in different mice, while CNK cells increased significantly in the liver of fibrotic mice compared with normal mice. Notably, IL-10 gene intervention further promoted the upregulation of CNK cells. We further compared the proportion of NK cells in the liver and peripheral blood of different mice and found that the intervention of IL-10 downregulated NK cells in peripheral blood and upregulated the number of NK cells in the liver. These results suggest that IL-10 enhances NK cell aggregation in fibrotic liver probably through the recruitment of CNK cells.

Revised paragraph:

The immune function of liver NK cells is closely linked to their development and source subpopulations. Based on developmental stages, they can be categorized as ink (cd27+cd11b-) with low cytotoxicity and high proliferation, TNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and high proliferation, and MNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and low proliferation. Our findings demonstrate that IL-10 gene intervention increased the frequency of TNK cells, suggesting a potential enhancement of overall NK cell cytotoxicity. Additionally, NK cells can be classified into two groups based on their sources: liver resident NK cells, critical for liver homeostasis and tissue repair, and peripheral circulating CNK cells, which migrate from the blood to the liver during inflammation or infection to exert cytotoxic activity. Our results indicate that the frequency of lrnk cells remained consistent among mice, while CNK cells significantly increased in the liver of fibrotic mice compared to normal mice. Significantly, IL-10 gene intervention further promoted the upregulation of CNK cells. Furthermore, a comparison of NK cell proportions in the liver and peripheral blood revealed that IL-10 intervention led to suppressed NK cells in peripheral blood while increasing their numbers in the liver. These findings suggest that IL-10 enhances NK cell aggregation in the fibrotic liver, potentially through the recruitment of CNK cells.

If you are a SCI paper author, help me revise this paragraph to make it more concise and logical：The immune function of liver NK cells is closely related to their development and source. Based on developmental stages, NK cells can be categorized as iNK (cd27+cd11b-) with low cytotoxicity and high proliferation, tNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and high proliferation, and mNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and low proliferation.  Our findings indicate that IL-10 gene intervention increased the frequency of tNK cells, potentially enhancing total NK cell cytotoxicity. Moreover, NK cells can be divided into two groups based on sources: LrNK cells, which maintain liver homeostasis and aid in tissue repair, and cNK cells, which migrate to the liver during inflammation to exert cytotoxic activity. It was observed that the frequency of lrnk cells remained unchanged among different mice, while the number of cNK cells significantly increased in the livers of fibrotic mice compared to the normal. Notably, IL-10 gene intervention further promoted the upregulation of cNK cells. Our further analysis revealed that IL-10 intervention led to a decrease in peripheral blood NK cells and an increase in liver NK cells. These results suggest that IL-10 enhances the aggregation of NK cells in the fibrotic liver likely through the recruitment of cNK cells.

Revised paragraph:

The immune function of liver NK cells is closely tied to their development and source. Based on developmental stages, NK cells can be categorized as iNK (cd27+cd11b-) with low cytotoxicity and high proliferation, tNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and high proliferation, and mNK (cd27+cd11b+) with high cytotoxicity and low proliferation. Our results indicate that IL-10 gene intervention increased the frequency of tNK cells, potentially enhancing overall NK cell cytotoxicity. NK cells can also be classified into two groups based on their sources: LrNK cells, which maintain liver homeostasis and aid in tissue repair, and cNK cells, which migrate to the liver during inflammation to exert cytotoxic activity. While the frequency of lrnk cells remained consistent among mice, the number of cNK cells significantly increased in the livers of fibrotic mice compared to normal mice. Notably, IL-10 gene intervention further enhanced the upregulation of cNK cells. Furthermore, our analysis revealed that IL-10 intervention led to a reduction in peripheral blood NK cells and an increase in liver NK cells. These findings suggest that the aggregation of NK cells in the fibrotic liver is likely enhanced by IL-10, possibly through the recruitment of cNK cells.

If you are a SCI paper author, help me revise this paragraph to make it more concise and logical：NK cells often migrate to specific sites in response to inflammation or tissue damage by recruitment signals sent by chemokines. The occurrence of liver fibrosis leads to the production of chemokines by various damaged cells and immune cells to participate in the progression and regression of fibrosis. In this study, we studied several chemokines, including CCL2, which mainly attracts monocytes, CCL3, which attracts various immune cells, including monocytes, eosinophils, and T cells, and CCL5, which is particularly attractive to NK cells. CCL5 can interact with CCR5 on the surface of NK cells, regulate the proliferation and activation of NK cells, and enhance the cytotoxicity and antiviral response of NK cells in chronic liver injury. Our results showed that CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5 were significantly increased in fibrotic livers compared with normal mice. However, IL-10 intervention mainly increased the level of CCL5 in the liver of fibrotic mice. Taken together, we speculate that IL-10 may promote the migration of CNK cells to fibrotic liver through CCL5.

Revised paragraph:

NK cells migrate to specific sites in response to inflammation or tissue damage, often guided by chemokines released as recruitment signals. Liver fibrosis triggers the production of chemokines by damaged cells and immune cells, contributing to the progression and regression of fibrosis. In this study, we focused on several chemokines, including CCL2, attracting monocytes; CCL3, attracting multiple immune cells such as monocytes, eosinophils, and T cells; and CCL5, especially attractive to NK cells. CCL5 interacts with CCR5 on NK cell surfaces, regulating their proliferation, activation, cytotoxicity, and antiviral response during chronic liver injury. Our findings revealed significant increases in CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5 levels in fibrotic livers compared to normal mice. Notably, IL-10 intervention predominantly increased the level of CCL5 in the liver of fibrotic mice. Together, these results suggest that IL-10 may enhance the migration of cNK cells to the fibrotic liver through the upregulation of CCL5 expression.

improve：NK cells often migrate to specific sites in response to inflammation or tissue damage by recruitment signals sent by chemokines. The occurrence of liver fibrosis leads to the production of chemokines by various damaged cells and immune cells to participate in the progression and regression of fibrosis. In this study, we studied several chemokines, including CCL2, which mainly attracts monocytes, CCL3, which attracts various immune cells, including monocytes, eosinophils, and T cells, and CCL5, which is particularly attractive to NK cells. CCL5 can interact with CCR5 on the surface of NK cells, regulate the proliferation and activation of NK cells, and enhance the cytotoxicity and antiviral response of NK cells in chronic liver injury. Our results showed that CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5 were significantly increased in fibrotic livers compared with normal mice. However, IL-10 intervention mainly increased the level of CCL5 in the liver of fibrotic mice. Taken together, we speculate that IL-10 may promote the migration of CNK cells to fibrotic liver through CCL5.

Revised paragraph:

NK cells are known to migrate to specific sites in response to inflammation or tissue damage, guided by chemokines released as recruitment signals. Liver fibrosis induces the production of chemokines by damaged cells and immune cells, influencing the progression and regression of fibrosis. In our study, we investigated several chemokines, including CCL2 (monocyte attractant), CCL3 (attracts various immune cells including monocytes, eosinophils, and T cells), and CCL5 (specifically attractive to NK cells). CCL5 interacts with CCR5 on NK cell surfaces, regulating their proliferation, activation, cytotoxicity, and antiviral response during chronic liver injury. Our findings revealed significant increases in CCL2, CCL3, and CCL5 levels in fibrotic livers compared to normal mice. Importantly, IL-10 intervention specifically increased the level of CCL5 in the fibrotic liver. Based on these observations, we speculate that IL-10 may facilitate the migration of cNK cells to the fibrotic liver through the upregulation of CCL5 expression.

improve：To investigate the importance of NK cells for the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10, we used asgm1 to clear functional NK cells in mice, and then re evaluated the anti fibrotic ability of IL-10. It was found that fibrotic mice lacking NK cells suffered from more severe inflammation and fibrosis. The anti-inflammatory and anti fibrotic effects of IL-10 gene intervention were significantly inhibited in the absence of NK cells. These results indicate the significant contribution of NK cells to the regression of liver fibrosis, and further emphasize the indispensable role of NK cells in the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10.

Revised paragraph:

To assess the relevance of NK cells in the antifibrotic effect of IL-10, we employed asgm1 to deplete functional NK cells in mice and subsequently reexamined the anti-fibrotic efficacy of IL-10. Strikingly, fibrotic mice lacking NK cells exhibited heightened inflammation and fibrosis. Furthermore, the anti-inflammatory and anti-fibrotic effects of IL-10 gene intervention were significantly attenuated in the absence of NK cells. These findings underscore the substantial contribution of NK cells to liver fibrosis regression and further substantiate their indispensable role in the antifibrotic mechanism exerted by IL-10.

improve：To investigate the importance of NK cells for the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10, we used asgm1 to clear functional NK cells from fibrotic mice in vivo, and then re evaluated liver pathology and function. It was found that fibrotic mice lacking NK cells suffered from more severe inflammation and fibrosis. The anti-inflammatory and anti fibrotic effects of IL-10 gene intervention were significantly inhibited in the absence of NK cells. These results indicate the significant contribution of NK cells to the regression of liver fibrosis, and further emphasize the indispensable role of NK cells in the anti fibrotic effect of IL-10.

Revised paragraph:

To assess the relevance of NK cells in the antifibrotic effect of IL-10, we utilized asgm1 to selectively deplete functional NK cells in fibrotic mice in vivo. Subsequently, we reevaluated liver pathology and function in these mice. Notably, fibrotic mice lacking NK cells exhibited exacerbated inflammation and fibrosis. Importantly, the anti-inflammatory and antifibrotic effects of IL-10 gene intervention were significantly hindered in the absence of NK cells. These findings highlight the substantial contribution of NK cells to liver fibrosis regression and underscore their indispensable role in mediating the antifibrotic effect of IL-10.
